title: The Fifteenth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: September 2, 2018

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Fifteenth Sunday after Pentecost falls within Ordinary Time, the longest liturgical season in the Christian calendar. Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings for this Sunday would likely be Deuteronomy 4:1-2, 6-9; Psalm 15; James 1:17-27; and Mark 7:1-8, 14-15, 21-23—texts focusing on observance of the law, true religion, and Jesus's teachings on what defiles a person. This sermon date fell two weeks before Labor Day, well into the post-Pentecost season before the approach of Advent.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the months following its General Convention held in Austin, Texas in July 2018, which had addressed various social justice resolutions and reaffirmed church positions on LGBTQ+ inclusion. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Robert O'Neill (who served 2008-2020), was engaged in its regular pastoral work; no major diocesan controversies or bishop elections were occurring at this time. The broader Anglican Communion was navigating ongoing tensions regarding sexuality and marriage, though these were not newly acute in September 2018.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing typical late summer weather with the transition toward fall beginning in early September. Colorado's political landscape was heating up with the midterm elections just two months away, with races for governor, Senate, and House seats drawing increasing attention. No major weather disasters or acute community crises dominated local news in the immediate week before September 2, though Denver's ongoing housing affordability crisis and growth management remained persistent civic concerns.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The dominant national story was the final week before the 2018 midterm elections campaign intensified, with the Trump administration's policies and the Republican and Democratic platforms shaping political discourse. The Senate confirmation hearings for Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh were generating major national controversy, with the hearings having begun in early September (the specific date of this sermon places it during the opening days of testimony). Hurricane Florence was developing in the Atlantic and would make landfall in North Carolina the following week, prompting evacuation orders and emergency preparations along the East Coast.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The war in Syria continued with ongoing Russian and Iranian involvement supporting the Assad regime, though major military operations were not at an acute peak at this specific moment. Italy's new populist government under Giuseppe Conte had just taken office in June 2018 and was implementing its anti-immigration agenda, creating tensions with the European Union. The Brexit negotiations between the UK and EU were ongoing in their second year, with Theresa May's government attempting to finalize a withdrawal agreement.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in early September 2018 reflected deep polarization and anxiety about the upcoming midterm elections, with many viewing the vote as a referendum on the Trump presidency. The #MeToo movement, which had gained massive momentum in late 2017, was at a sustained cultural peak, and the Kavanaugh hearings were amplifying discussions about sexual assault and consent. The NFL season was about to begin, and Denver's Broncos were a point of local sports interest, though the team was in a rebuilding phase following Peyton Manning's retirement.
